## Per-question answers

### `-7013890438520559398`
> **Pelé (1281 goals recognized by FIFA) or Josef Bican (805-1468 goals depending on source)**
> Pelé's 1281 goals are recognized by FIFA as the highest total achieved by a professional footballer. [alce::-7013890438520559398::p0]
> However, the Soccer Statistic Foundation (RSSSF) recognizes only 767 goals in official mode for Pelé, occupying the third place after Josef Bican (805) and Romario (772). [alce::-7013890438520559398::p0]
> Josef Bican scored at least 805 goals in all competitive matches according to RSSSF, which would make him the most prolific scorer of all time, and the IFFHS awarded him the 'Golden Ball' as the greatest goalscorer of the last century with at least 1468 goals overall. [alce::-7013890438520559398::p1, alce::-7013890438520559398::p2]

Cited: alce::-7013890438520559398::p0, alce::-7013890438520559398::p1, alce::-7013890438520559398::p2

### `7089015503030534342`
> **Simon & Garfunkel**
> "The Sound of Silence", originally "The Sounds of Silence", is a song by the American music duo Simon & Garfunkel. [alce::7089015503030534342::p0]
> The song was written by Paul Simon over a period of several months in 1963 and 1964. [alce::7089015503030534342::p0]

### `-881464876144297194`
> **James and Oliver Phelps played Fred and George Weasley; Domhnall Gleeson played Bill Weasley; Chris Rankin played Percy Weasley**
> James and Oliver Phelps are identical twin British actors, best known for playing Fred and George Weasley in the 'Harry Potter' film series. [alce::-881464876144297194::p1]
> Domhnall Gleeson plays Bill Weasley in 'Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows' and the roller coaster ride 'Harry Potter and the Escape from Gringotts' at The Wizarding World of Harry Potter. [alce::-881464876144297194::p0]
> Percy is portrayed by Chris Rankin in the films. [alce::-881464876144297194::p4]
> Rupert Grint portrays Ron Weasley. [alce::-881464876144297194::p3]

### `6962706727076805207`
> **A bait car (also called a decoy car, hot car, or trap car)**
> A bait car, also called a decoy car, hot car or trap car, is a vehicle used by law enforcement agencies to capture car thieves or thieves who steal items from cars. [alce::6962706727076805207::p0]
> The vehicles are modified with audio/video surveillance technology, and can be remotely monitored and controlled. [alce::6962706727076805207::p0]

### `4965896127144803833`
> **Epidemic diseases (particularly smallpox) and internal civil conflict weakened the Inca Empire, facilitating Spanish conquest**
> The Andean people had been isolated for millennia and therefore had no reason to build up any sort of immunity against foreign diseases, which meant that the introduction of a non-native population had the potential to spell disaster for the Andeans. [alce::4965896127144803833::p0]
> The spread of smallpox greatly weakened the Inca empire, even before the Spanish arrival, because the Amerindians did not have as much natural immunity to the disease as did the Europeans who had been exposed to smallpox for roughly two centuries. [alce::4965896127144803833::p1]
> The disease killed Inca ruler Wayna Capac, triggering a civil war in the Inca empire that preceded the conquest efforts the Spaniards, and the conquest was facilitated by the weakness of the empire. [alce::4965896127144803833::p1]
> Smallpox spread rapidly before the Spanish invaders first arrived in the empire, probably aided by the efficient Inca road system, and was followed by other epidemics including Typhus, influenza, diphtheria, and measles that ravaged the Inca people. [alce::4965896127144803833::p2]
> The Inca population declined from millions in the 1520s to around 600,000 in 1620 mainly because of infectious diseases, with epidemic disease such as smallpox being the overwhelming cause of the population decline. [alce::4965896127144803833::p4]

Cited: alce::4965896127144803833::p0, alce::4965896127144803833::p1, alce::4965896127144803833::p2, alce::4965896127144803833::p4

### `-5001543574321430501`
> **Chicago blues (electric blues style of urban blues)**
> Chicago blues is an electric blues style of urban blues that evolved from classic blues following the Great Migration of African Americans from the southern United States to industrial cities of the north, such as Chicago. [alce::-5001543574321430501::p3]
> Many Delta blues artists moved to Chicago, creating a pop-influenced city blues style, which was displaced by the new Chicago blues sound in the early 1950s, pioneered by Delta bluesmen Muddy Waters, Howlin' Wolf and Little Walter, featuring amplified instruments. [alce::-5001543574321430501::p2]

> **The crust and lithosphere are the solid parts of Earth**
> The internal structure of the Earth is layered in spherical shells: an outer silicate solid crust, a highly viscous asthenosphere and mantle, a liquid outer core that is much less viscous than the mantle, and a solid inner core. [alce::3189494571253889631::p3, alce::3189494571253889631::p4]
> The crust and the cold, rigid, top of the upper mantle are collectively known as the lithosphere, and it is of the lithosphere that the tectonic plates are composed. [alce::3189494571253889631::p1]

### `-1528483370616468286`
> **The authorship of the first five books of Moses is disputed; traditionally attributed to Moses, but modern scholars conclude it was written by someone else who lived long after him**
> Traditionally, the first five books of the Bible (the Pentateuch) were believed to have been written by Moses, though some scholars noted that the description of Moses' death must have been written by someone else. [alce::-1528483370616468286::p0]
> However, beginning in the 17th century, scholars began to seriously question the Pentateuch's origins, with Benedict Spinoza declaring that 'the Pentateuch was not written by Moses but by someone else.' [alce::-1528483370616468286::p0]
> Spinoza concluded that the Pentateuch was written by someone who lived long after Moses, not by Moses himself. [alce::-1528483370616468286::p1, alce::-1528483370616468286::p2]
> By Hellenistic times, Jewish writers referred to the entirety of the five books as the Book (or books) of Moses, though authorship was not considered important by the society that produced the Hebrew Bible. [alce::-1528483370616468286::p3]

Cited: alce::-1528483370616468286::p0, alce::-1528483370616468286::p1, alce::-1528483370616468286::p2, alce::-1528483370616468286::p3

### `5881756658805354957`
> **Ancient Chinese civilization began around 1900-1500 BCE (Erlitou period) and ended in 221 BCE with the unification under the Qin dynasty**
> Chinese civilization begins during the second phase of the Erlitou period (1900 to 1500 BC), with Erlitou considered the first state level society of East Asia. [alce::5881756658805354957::p4]
> The Warring States period concluded with the Qin wars of conquest that saw the annexation of all other contender states, which ultimately led to the Qin state's victory in 221 BC as the first unified Chinese empire, known as the Qin dynasty. [alce::5881756658805354957::p1]
> The four centuries preceding the unification of China in 221 BC (the later Spring and Autumn period and the Warring States period) constitute the Chinese classical period in the strict sense. [alce::5881756658805354957::p2]

> It was erected in 1892–1893 to commemorate the philanthropic works of Lord Shaftesbury, who was a famous Victorian politician and philanthropist. [alce::2810825654363491960::p0]

### `-6359539096826078233`
> **Music likely existed for at least 50,000 years, possibly originating in Africa**
> Since all people of the world, including the most isolated tribal groups, have a form of music, it may be concluded that music is likely to have been present in the ancestral population prior to the dispersal of humans around the world. [alce::-6359539096826078233::p2]
> Consequently, music may have been in existence for at least 50,000 years and the first music may have been invented in Africa and then evolved to become a fundamental constituent of human life. [alce::-6359539096826078233::p2]
> Music can be theoretically traced to prior to the Paleolithic age, the anthropological and archaeological designation suggests that music first arose (among humans) when stone tools first began to be used by hominids. [alce::-6359539096826078233::p1]

Cited: alce::-6359539096826078233::p1, alce::-6359539096826078233::p2

### `-8177486160066957979`
> **To direct traffic flow in one direction around a central island while improving safety and reducing collisions**
> A traffic circle is a type of intersection that directs both turning and through traffic onto a one-way circular roadway, usually built for the purposes of traffic calming or aesthetics. [alce::-8177486160066957979::p3]
> Modern roundabouts reduce the likelihood and severity of collisions by reducing traffic speeds and minimizing T-bone and head-on collisions. [alce::-8177486160066957979::p0]
> Traffic moves slowly enough to allow visual engagement with pedestrians, encouraging deference towards them, and they reduce driver confusion associated with perpendicular junctions. [alce::-8177486160066957979::p2]

### `9135439235777717832`
> **The ophthalmic nerve exits the skull through the superior orbital fissure**
> The ophthalmic, maxillary and mandibular branches leave the skull through three separate foramina: the superior orbital fissure, the foramen rotundum and the foramen ovale, respectively. [alce::9135439235777717832::p2]
> A branch passes through the orbit superiorly, then reenters the frontal bone briefly before exiting above the orbit through the superior orbital fissure and the supraorbital notch to provide sensory innervation for the skin of the forehead and scalp. [alce::9135439235777717832::p0]

### `1719387094791600068`
> **Skeletal muscle fibers innervated by a single motor neuron's axonal terminals**
> A motor unit is made up of a motor neuron and the skeletal muscle fibers innervated by that motor neuron's axonal terminals. [alce::1719387094791600068::p2]
> Skeletal muscles are organized into hundreds of motor units, each of which involves a motor neuron, attached by a series of thin finger-like structures called axon terminals that attach to and control discrete bundles of muscle fibers. [alce::1719387094791600068::p3]
> When a motor unit is activated, all of its fibres contract. [alce::1719387094791600068::p2]

### `8216960630679687356`
> **John O'Sullivan coined the phrase "manifest destiny" in 1845. It means the belief that the United States was destined to expand across North America and rule the entire North American continent.**
> Newspaper editor John O'Sullivan is generally credited with coining the term 'manifest destiny' in 1845 to describe the essence of this mindset, which was a rhetorical tone. [alce::8216960630679687356::p0]
> In the 19th century, manifest destiny was a widely held belief in the United States that its settlers were destined to expand across North America. [alce::8216960630679687356::p2]
> Manifest destiny was an expression of conviction in the morality and value of expansionism that complemented other popular ideas of the era, including American exceptionalism and Romantic nationalism. [alce::8216960630679687356::p1]
> The term captured the idea that the young American nation was destined to rule all of the North American continent. [alce::8216960630679687356::p3]

Cited: alce::8216960630679687356::p0, alce::8216960630679687356::p1, alce::8216960630679687356::p2, alce::8216960630679687356::p3

### `-7777910189889414373`
> **The Paschal candle is lit at Easter, specifically at the Easter Vigil on Holy Saturday night, and throughout the year at baptisms and funerals**
> A new Paschal candle is blessed and lit every year at Easter, and is used throughout the Paschal season which is during Easter and then throughout the year on special occasions, such as baptisms and funerals. [alce::-7777910189889414373::p0]
> For churches that celebrate the Easter Vigil on the night of Holy Saturday, the ceremonial lighting of the Paschal candle is one of the most solemn moments of the service. [alce::-7777910189889414373::p2]
> The Paschal candle is also lit during baptisms to signify the Holy Spirit and fire that John the Baptist promised to those who were baptised in Christ. [alce::-7777910189889414373::p4]
> The Paschal candle is also lit and placed near the casket or urn during funeral services such as the Mass of Repose and the [alce::-7777910189889414373::p4]

> The Bogardus social distance scale is a psychological testing scale created by Emory S. Bogardus to empirically measure people's willingness to participate in social contacts of varying degrees of closeness with members of diverse social groups. [alce::-9150237805953541495::p3]

### `-2777825999784077693`
> **Karl Landsteiner**
> Austrian Physician Karl Landsteiner working at the Pathological-Anatomical Institute of the University of Vienna discovered the way to determine blood types. [alce::-2777825999784077693::p0]
> In 1900, he found that blood sera from different persons would clump together (agglutinate) when mixed in test tubes, and the next year in 1901, he made a definitive observation that blood serum of an individual would agglutinate with only those of certain individuals. [alce::-2777825999784077693::p0]
> Based on this he classified human bloods into three groups, which were later identified as O, A, and B blood types. [alce::-2777825999784077693::p3]

Cited: alce::-2777825999784077693::p0, alce::-2777825999784077693::p3

### `4537952778249650608`
> **Polar regions (Arctic Circle and Antarctic Circle, around 66.5° latitude)**
> At the Equator, the daytime period always lasts about 12 hours, regardless of season, meaning there is minimal annual change in daylight hours. [alce::4537952778249650608::p1]
> At middle latitudes, variations in the length of daytime are moderate, with higher middle latitudes showing very noticeable differences between summer and winter. [alce::4537952778249650608::p0]
> Polar areas experience the greatest annual change in daylight, with places on the Arctic Circle having the longest total annual daytime of 4,647 hours and experiencing 24 hours of summer daylight (midnight sun) contrasted with winter darkness. [alce::4537952778249650608::p2, alce::4537952778249650608::p4]
> The Arctic Circle and Antarctic Circle, which mark the 66.5° north or south latitude boundaries, define where the phenomenon of continuous summer daylight and winter darkness occurs. [alce::4537952778249650608::p4]

Cited: alce::4537952778249650608::p0, alce::4537952778249650608::p1, alce::4537952778249650608::p2, alce::4537952778249650608::p4

### `-3149241670475079084`
> **Peanut butter and jelly originated as a sandwich combination in the late 1800s and early 1900s, with recipes published in magazines like "Good Housekeeping" (1896) and "Table Talk" (1896), becoming popular with children by the 1920s as manufacturers added sugar to peanut butter.**
> In a 'Good Housekeeping' article published in May 1896, a recipe urged homemakers to use a meat grinder to make peanut butter and spread the result on bread. [alce::-3149241670475079084::p2]
> The following month, the culinary magazine 'Table Talk' published a peanut butter sandwich recipe. [alce::-3149241670475079084::p2]
> In the early 1900s, this sandwich was adopted down the class structure as the price of peanut butter dropped. [alce::-3149241670475079084::p2]
> It became popular with children by the 1920s as manufacturers began adding sugar to the peanut butter. [alce::-3149241670475079084::p2]
> Since World War II, both peanut butter and jelly were found on US soldiers' military ration list. [alce::-3149241670475079084::p2]

Cited: alce::-3149241670475079084::p2

### `7030752080582135428`
> **1662 - Christopher Merret's documented method for deliberately producing sparkling wine**
> In 1662, the English scientist Christopher Merret presented a paper detailing how the presence of sugar in a wine led to it eventually sparkling, and that nearly any wine could be made to sparkle by adding sugar to a wine before bottling it. [alce::7030752080582135428::p2]
> The first documented evidence of a deliberately produced sparkling wine is recorded by Christopher Merret, who presented his eight-page paper 'Some observations concerning the ordering of wines' to the Royal Society on December 17, 1662, stating that wine-coopers use sugar and molasses to make wines drink brisk and sparkling. [alce::7030752080582135428::p3]
> The English scientist and physician Christopher Merret documented the addition of sugar to a finished wine to create a second fermentation, six years before Dom Pérignon set foot in the Abbey of Hautvillers, presenting his paper at the Royal Society in 1662. [alce::7030752080582135428::p4]

Cited: alce::7030752080582135428::p2, alce::7030752080582135428::p3, alce::7030752080582135428::p4

### `-1233203944270329571`
> **Cædmon's Hymn (7th century)**
> "Cædmon's Hymn", composed in the 7th century, according to Bede, is often considered the oldest extant poem in English. [alce::-1233203944270329571::p0]
> Cædmon is the earliest English poet whose name is known, and Cædmon's only known surviving work is "Cædmon's Hymn", which probably dates before AD 1000. [alce::-1233203944270329571::p1]
> Some of the most important surviving works of Old English literature are "Beowulf", an epic poem; the "Anglo-Saxon Chronicle", a record of early English history; the Franks Casket, an inscribed early whalebone artefact; and Cædmon's Hymn, a Christian religious poem. [alce::-1233203944270329571::p2]

Cited: alce::-1233203944270329571::p0, alce::-1233203944270329571::p1, alce::-1233203944270329571::p2

### `4210365122385001657`
> **At the end of ventricular systole, when the aortic and pulmonary valves close**
> The second heart sound, or S2, forms the 'dub' of 'lub-dub' and is composed of components A (aortic valve closure) and P (pulmonary valve closure). [alce::4210365122385001657::p0]
> At the end of ventricular systole, when the pressure in the right ventricle falls rapidly, the pressure in the pulmonary artery will close the pulmonary valve. [alce::4210365122385001657::p1]
> The closure of the pulmonary valve contributes the P2 component of the second heart sound. [alce::4210365122385001657::p1]

Cited: alce::4210365122385001657::p0, alce::4210365122385001657::p1

### `7276771340691790412`
> **Tim Paterson**
> MS-DOS was a renamed form of 86-DOS owned by Seattle Computer Products, written by Tim Paterson. [alce::7276771340691790412::p1]
> Tim Paterson had developed a variant of CP/M-80, intended as an internal product for testing SCP's new 16-bit Intel 8086 CPU card for the S-100 bus, which was initially named QDOS (Quick and Dirty Operating System), before being made commercially available as 86-DOS. [alce::7276771340691790412::p2]
> Microsoft purchased 86-DOS and it was modified for the IBM PC by Microsoft employee Bob O'Rear with assistance from SCP (later Microsoft) employee Tim Paterson. [alce::7276771340691790412::p4]

### `8307493951745307544`
> **Ferdinand Magellan led the first voyage to circumnavigate the globe**
> The first single voyage of global circumnavigation was that of the ship 'Victoria', between 1519 and 1522, known as the Magellan–Elcano expedition, led initially by the Portuguese Ferdinand Magellan between 1519 and 1521. [alce::8307493951745307544::p0]
> The first circumnavigation of the earth was completed in 1522 with the Magellan-Elcano expedition, a Spanish voyage of discovery led by Portuguese explorer Ferdinand Magellan and completed by Spanish navigator Juan Sebastián Elcano after the former's death in the Philippines in 1521. [alce::8307493951745307544::p1]
> The first direct demonstration of Earth's sphericity came in the form of the first circumnavigation in history, an expedition captained by Portuguese explorer Ferdinand Magellan. [alce::8307493951745307544::p3]

Cited: alce::8307493951745307544::p0, alce::8307493951745307544::p1, alce::8307493951745307544::p3

### `8020759438851523056`
> **1958 - The federal student loan program started in 1958 under the National Defense Education Act (NDEA)**
> United States Government-backed student loans were first offered in 1958 under the National Defense Education Act (NDEA), and were only available to select categories of students, such as those studying toward engineering, science, or education degrees. [alce::8020759438851523056::p1]
> The student loan program, along with other parts of the Act, which subsidized college professor training, was established in response to the Soviet Union's launch of the Sputnik satellite, and a widespread perception that the United States was falling behind in science and technology. [alce::8020759438851523056::p1]

Cited: alce::8020759438851523056::p1

### `7972203314749871207`
> **Change the number of protons in the lead nucleus through nuclear transmutation**
> Nuclear transmutation is the conversion of one chemical element or an isotope into another chemical element. [alce::7972203314749871207::p4]
> Because any element (or isotope of one) is defined by its number of protons (and neutrons) in its atoms, i.e. in the atomic nucleus, nuclear transmutation occurs in any process where the number of protons or neutrons in the nucleus is changed. [alce::7972203314749871207::p4]
> A transmutation can be achieved either by nuclear reactions (in which an outside particle reacts with a nucleus) or by radioactive decay, where no outside cause is needed. [alce::7972203314749871207::p4]
> Nuclear experiments have successfully transmuted lead into gold, but the expense far exceeds any gain. [alce::7972203314749871207::p1]

Cited: alce::7972203314749871207::p1, alce::7972203314749871207::p4

### `6630776554188306922`
> **The smell of vanilla comes from the vanilla orchid fruit (seed pod), which releases a distinctive vanilla smell as it dries and the phenolic compounds crystallize.**
> Vanilla is a flavoring derived from orchids of the genus 'Vanilla', primarily from the Mexican species, flat-leaved vanilla ('V. planifolia'). [alce::6630776554188306922::p1]
> The fruit, a seed capsule, if left on the plant, ripens and opens at the end; as it dries, the phenolic compounds crystallize, giving the fruits a diamond-dusted appearance, which the French call 'givre' (hoarfrost). It then releases the distinctive vanilla smell. [alce::6630776554188306922::p0]
> The main aroma and flavor components of vanilla are vanillin and 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde. [alce::6630776554188306922::p4]

Cited: alce::6630776554188306922::p0, alce::6630776554188306922::p1, alce::6630776554188306922::p4

### `6945133188536552979`
> **The Roman Republic and Medieval Spain (León)**
> The Roman Republic was the first known government in the western world to have a representative government, despite taking the form of a direct government in the Roman assemblies. [alce::6945133188536552979::p0]
> In 1188 Alfonso IX, King of Leon (in current day Spain) convened the three states in the Cortes of León and according to UNESCO it was the first sample of modern parliamentarism in the history of Europe. [alce::6945133188536552979::p2]
> Keane locates the birth of representative parliaments in the cloisters of San Isidoro Church, in León, Northern Spain - the site where King Alfonso IX convened the first cortes in 1188 CE. [alce::6945133188536552979::p4]

Cited: alce::6945133188536552979::p0, alce::6945133188536552979::p2, alce::6945133188536552979::p4

[alce::-1763300345435461044::p4]

### `6990604505432466959`
> **Pax Romana means "Roman Peace" and is important as a period of relative peace and stability that allowed the Roman Empire to achieve its greatest territorial extent and population, while also becoming highly influential as a concept that was imitated in later periods.**
> Pax Romana is Latin for 'Roman Peace' and was a long period of relative peace and stability experienced by the Roman Empire between the accession of Caesar Augustus and the death of Marcus Aurelius. [alce::6990604505432466959::p0]
> During this period of approximately 206 years (27 BC to AD 180), the Roman empire achieved its greatest territorial extent and its population reached a maximum of up to 70 million. [alce::6990604505432466959::p0]
> The concept of Pax Romana was highly influential, and attempts to imitate it occurred in the Byzantine Empire and in the Christian West, where it morphed into the Peace and Truce of God. [alce::6990604505432466959::p1]

Cited: alce::6990604505432466959::p0, alce::6990604505432466959::p1

> **The Romano-Britons expelled the Romans from Britain around 410 AD**

> Regarding the events of 409 and 410 when the Romano-Britons expelled Roman officials and sent a request for aid to Honorius, Michael Jones suggested that the Britons first appealed to Rome and when no help was forthcoming, they expelled the Roman officials and took charge of their own affairs. ### `3592757457733086904`
> **1827**
> New York became a free state on July 4, 1827, when the last slaves in the state were emancipated. [alce::3592757457733086904::p0, alce::3592757457733086904::p2]
> The law passed in 1817 would finally emancipate slaves, but it did not take effect for ten years, and when it finally took effect, the last 2,800 slaves in New York State were emancipated on July 4, 1827. [alce::3592757457733086904::p0]
> It was not until 1827 that slavery was completely abolished in the state. [alce::3592757457733086904::p3]

### `555917789511232078`
> **Yes, there is a fee to get a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame**
> In 1980 a fee of $2,500 was instituted, payable by the person or entity nominating the recipient, to fund the Walk of Fame's upkeep. [alce::555917789511232078::p0]
> The fee has increased incrementally over time; by 2002 it had reached $15,000, and stood at $30,000 in 2012, with the current (2017) fee being $40,000. [alce::555917789511232078::p0]
> On February 9, 2007, the Hollywood Chamber Of Commerce raised the price to sponsor a new star to $25,000, and in December 2010, the price was raised again to $30,000, and in 2017 the price was raised to $40,000. [alce::555917789511232078::p2]

### `223527143081061606`
> **Most taste buds are located on the upper surface of the tongue, embedded in papillae (fungiform, circumvallate, and foliate papillae)**
> The taste receptors are located around the small structures known as papillae found on the upper surface of the tongue, soft palate, upper esophagus, the cheek, and epiglottis. [alce::223527143081061606::p0]
> The upper surface of the tongue is covered in masticatory mucosa and embedded in this are numerous papillae that house the taste buds and their taste receptors. [alce::223527143081061606::p2]
> All except the filiform papillae are associated with taste buds, meaning fungiform, circumvallate (vallate), and foliate papillae contain taste buds. [alce::223527143081061606::p3]

Cited: alce::223527143081061606::p0, alce::223527143081061606::p2, alce::223527143081061606::p3

### `-1217712158413261548`
> **Denitrification**
> Denitrification is the reduction of nitrate and nitrite to the gaseous form of nitrogen, including the greenhouse gas nitrous oxide, that is released back into the atmosphere. [alce::-1217712158413261548::p1]
> Denitrification is the reduction of nitrates back into nitrogen gas (N), completing the nitrogen cycle. [alce::-1217712158413261548::p2]
> This process is performed by bacterial species such as 'Pseudomonas' and 'Clostridium' in anaerobic conditions. [alce::-1217712158413261548::p2]

### `-4508291966620018192`
> **The highest temperatures on Earth are recorded in Death Valley, California (Furnace Creek Ranch), with the official WMO record of 54°C (129.2°F) on July 10, 1913, though this is disputed. Ground surface temperatures are highest in the Lut Desert, Iran, and satellite measurements show the highest temperatures in the Flaming Mountains of China.**
> According to the World Meteorological Organization (WMO), the highest registered air temperature on Earth was in Furnace Creek Ranch, California, located in the Death Valley desert in the United States, on July 10, 1913. [alce::-4508291966620018192::p1]
> The Lut Desert in Iran was found to have the highest maximum temperature in 5 of the 7 years measured (2004, 2005, 2006, 2007 and 2009) when measuring regional surface temperatures. [alce::-4508291966620018192::p0]
> A ground temperature of 93.9 °C (201 °F) was recorded in Furnace Creek, Death Valley, California, United States on 15 July 1972, which may be the highest natural ground surface temperature ever recorded. [alce::-4508291966620018192::p2]
> The highest recorded temperature taken by a satellite is in the Flaming Mountains of China in 2008. [alce::-4508291966620018192::p3]

Cited: alce::-4508291966620018192::p0, alce::-4508291966620018192::p1, alce::-4508291966620018192::p2, alce::-4508291966620018192::p3

### `-3243271997177308524`
> **Proteases, Carbohydrases (Glycoside Hydrolases), and Lipases**
> Enzymes such as amylases and proteases break down large molecules (starch or proteins, respectively) into smaller ones, so they can be absorbed by the intestines. [alce::-3243271997177308524::p1]
> These digestive enzymes include proteases that digest proteins into amino acids, as well as glycoside hydrolases that digest polysaccharides into simple sugars known as monosaccharides. [alce::-3243271997177308524::p2]
> Digestion of some fats can begin in the mouth where lingual lipase breaks down some short chain lipids into diglycerides. However fats are mainly digested in the small intestine. [alce::-3243271997177308524::p4]

### `-2427005357321258325`
> **Larger states**
> The Virginia Plan allocated representation to each state proportional to its population, meaning larger states would have more representatives and thus more power in the legislature. [alce::-2427005357321258325::p0, alce::-2427005357321258325::p2]
> States with a large population, like Virginia (which was the most populous state at the time), would thus have more representatives than smaller states. [alce::-2427005357321258325::p2]
> Large states supported this plan, and smaller states generally opposed it. [alce::-2427005357321258325::p2]

Cited: alce::-2427005357321258325::p0, alce::-2427005357321258325::p2

### `16881061444712536`
> **The Amazon River starts in the Andes Mountains of Peru (specifically in the Mantaro drainage near Mt. Mismi) and finishes by discharging into the Atlantic Ocean in Brazil.**
> The most distant point in the Mantaro drainage is nearly 80 km farther upstream compared to Mt. Mismi in the Apurímac drainage, and thus the maximal length of the Amazon river is about 80 km longer than previously thought, with the source identified in the Mantaro drainage. [alce::16881061444712536::p4]
> The Amazon enters Brazil and ultimately discharges into the Atlantic Ocean. [alce::16881061444712536::p1]
> The Amazon proper runs mostly through Brazil and Peru, and is part of the border between Colombia and Perú. [alce::16881061444712536::p0]

### `-5942352520455511076`
> **Adipose tissue can be found in multiple locations throughout the body including beneath the skin, around internal organs, in bone marrow, between muscles, and in breast tissue**
> In humans, adipose tissue is located: beneath the skin (subcutaneous fat), around internal organs (visceral fat), in bone marrow (yellow bone marrow), intermuscular (Muscular system) and in the breast tissue. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p4]
> In the integumentary system, which includes the skin, it accumulates in the deepest level, the subcutaneous layer, providing insulation from heat and cold. Around organs, it provides protective padding. [alce::-5942352520455511076::p1]
> Visceral fat or abdominal fat (also known as organ fat or intra-abdominal fat) is located inside the abdominal cavity, packed between the organs (stomach, liver, intestines, kidneys, etc.). [alce::-5942352520455511076::p3]

Cited: alce::-5942352520455511076::p1, alce::-5942352520455511076::p3, alce::-5942352520455511076::p4

### `-596092551637359468`
> **France was occupied by Germany from June 1940 to December 1944**

### `2547299592085156135`
> **Electrons in a covalent bond are located between atoms, shared between them**
> Pairs of electrons located between atoms represent covalent bonds. [alce::2547299592085156135::p0]
> A covalent bond is a chemical bond that involves the sharing of electron pairs between atoms. [alce::2547299592085156135::p1]
> These electron pairs are known as shared pairs or bonding pairs, and the stable balance of attractive and repulsive forces between atoms, when they share electrons, is known as covalent bonding. [alce::2547299592085156135::p1]

### `4653868131958586810`
> **Network Address Translation - a method of remapping IP address spaces by modifying network address information in IP packet headers**
> Network address translation (NAT) is a method of remapping one IP address space into another by modifying network address information in the IP header of packets while they are in transit across a traffic routing device. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]
> It has become a popular and essential tool in conserving global address space in the face of IPv4 address exhaustion. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]
> One Internet-routable IP address of a NAT gateway can be used for an entire private network. [alce::4653868131958586810::p0]

Cited: alce::4653868131958586810::p0

### `2188695242961419104`
> **32 teams in the group stage**
> In its present format, the UEFA Champions League begins in late June with four knockout qualifying rounds and a play-off round. The 6 surviving teams enter the group stage, joining 26 teams qualified in advance. The 32 teams are drawn into eight groups of four teams and play each other in a double round-robin system. [alce::2188695242961419104::p0]
> Since 2009–10 season, the UEFA Champions League begins with a double round-robin group stage of 32 teams, which is preceded by two qualification 'streams' for teams that do not receive direct entry to the tournament proper. [alce::2188695242961419104::p1]

### `-285728796551513770`
> **The Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox split due to theological and political differences, including doctrinal issues like the filioque clause and papal authority, exacerbated by cultural and linguistic differences between Latins and Greeks**
> The Schism was the culmination of theological and political differences between the Christian East and West which had developed over the preceding centuries. [alce::-285728796551513770::p0]
> There were doctrinal issues like the filioque clause and the authority of the Pope involved in the split, but these were exacerbated by cultural and linguistic differences between Latins and Greeks. [alce::-285728796551513770::p1]
> The formal break, known as the East–West Schism, came in 1054, when the papacy and the patriarchy of Constantinople clashed over papal supremacy and excommunicated each other. [alce::-285728796551513770::p2]

Cited: alce::-285728796551513770::p0, alce::-285728796551513770::p1, alce::-285728796551513770::p2

### `-8080892130396390822`
> **The phrase originated with Frederick the Great of Prussia and was popularized through various literary works and famous speeches**
> The first recorded use of a related phrase is by Frederick the Great of Prussia. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p0]
> The statement that 'Dog is man's best friend' was first recorded as being made by Frederick, King of Prussia (1740-1786), who referred to one of his Italian Greyhounds as his best friend. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p1]
> The earliest citation in the U.S. is traced to a poem printed in 'The New-York Literary Journal, Volume 4', 1821. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p1]
> It was likely popularized by its use in a poem by Ogden Nash. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p0]
> In 1870, George Graham Vest's closing speech in a legal case included the famous quote about a dog being man's best friend, and in 1958, a statue of Old Drum was erected with a summation of Vest's closing speech. [alce::-8080892130396390822::p1, alce::-8080892130396390822::p2]

### `-3529909367855134284`
> **Continental crust and oceanic crust**
> The crust of the Earth is of two distinctive types: continental crust and oceanic crust. [alce::-3529909367855134284::p0, alce::-3529909367855134284::p2]
> The thin parts are the oceanic crust, which underlie the ocean basins (5–10 km) and are composed of dense (mafic) iron magnesium silicate igneous rocks, like basalt. [alce::-3529909367855134284::p1]
> The thicker crust is continental crust, which is less dense and composed of (felsic) sodium potassium aluminium silicate rocks, like granite. [alce::-3529909367855134284::p1]

> Mary Warren has a very weak character, giving in to pressure a number of times. [alce::926074333625643859::p0]

> **1854 until the mid-1890s**
> The Third Party System is a term of periodization used by historians and political scientists to describe the history of political parties in the United States from 1854 until the mid-1890s, which featured profound developments in issues of American nationalism, modernization, and race. ### `-8300695731604896849`
> **Multiple pioneers developed the first alternating current electric system, with Hippolyte Pixii creating the first alternator in 1832, and Sebastian Ziani de Ferranti building the first practical AC power system in 1886**
> The first alternator to produce alternating current was a dynamo electric generator based on Michael Faraday's principles constructed by the French instrument maker Hippolyte Pixii in 1832. [alce::-8300695731604896849::p1]
> In 1832, French instrument maker Hippolyte Pixii generated a crude form of alternating current when he designed and built the first alternator, which consisted of a revolving horseshoe magnet passing over two wound-wire coils. [alce::-8300695731604896849::p2]
> Sebastian Ziani de Ferranti built the world's first practical transformer based alternating current power system at Great Barrington, Massachusetts in 1886. [alce::-8300695731604896849::p3]

### `-7301225247880986147`
> **The Great Wall of China started building during the Warring States period, with the earliest recorded construction by the State of Qi in 685 BCE, and was later unified and expanded by Qin Shi Huang in 221 BCE**
> In 685 BCE, the state of Qi started to build the Great Wall of Qi across Changqing county. [alce::-7301225247880986147::p0]
> During the Warring States period, the states of Qin, Wei, Zhao, Qi, Yan, and Zhongshan all constructed extensive fortifications to defend their own borders. [alce::-7301225247880986147::p4]
> The Great Wall of China had been built since the Qin dynasty (221–207 BC), although its present form was mostly an engineering feat and remodelling of the Ming dynasty (1368–1644 AD). [alce::-7301225247880986147::p1]
> Of these walls, those of the northern states Yan, Zhao, and Qin were connected by Qin Shi Huang when he united the Chinese states in 221 BC. [alce::-7301225247880986147::p2]

### `305726888559328341`
> **Metabolic activity occurs in the cytoplasm and its compartments, particularly the cytosol and mitochondria**
> Most cellular activities take place within the cytoplasm, such as many metabolic pathways including glycolysis, and processes such as cell division. [alce::305726888559328341::p0]
> The majority of both metabolic processes and metabolites occur in the cytosol, with major metabolic pathways including protein biosynthesis, the pentose phosphate pathway, glycolysis and gluconeogenesis. [alce::305726888559328341::p1]
> The citric acid cycle, electron transport chain, and oxidative phosphorylation all take place in the mitochondrial membrane, while glycolysis, pentose phosphate pathway, and fatty acid biosynthesis all occur in the cytosol of a cell. [alce::305726888559328341::p2]

Cited: alce::305726888559328341::p0, alce::305726888559328341::p1, alce::305726888559328341::p2

### `7803488668042298223`
> **Cupboard love originates from a psychological learning theory based on the concept that infants develop attachment through food gratification**
> Cupboard love is a popular learning theory of the 1950s and 1960s based on the research of Sigmund Freud, Anna Freud, Melanie Klein and Mary Ainsworth, rooted in psychoanalysis, which speculates that attachment develops in the early stages of infancy through the mother satisfying her infant's instinctual needs. [alce::7803488668042298223::p1]
> Cupboard love theorists conclude that during infancy, our primary drive is food which leads to a secondary drive for attachment. [alce::7803488668042298223::p0]
> The term "cupboard love" is a British idiom, used most commonly in the United Kingdom and Australia, describing the selfish, greedy or insincere affection displayed towards another person in order to get what they want. [alce::7803488668042298223::p2]

Cited: alce::7803488668042298223::p0, alce::7803488668042298223::p1, alce::7803488668042298223::p2

### `-4561791635646149573`
> **The first US immigration laws were passed in the 1790s, with the Naturalization Act of 1790 being the first naturalization law, followed by the Alien and Sedition Acts of the 1790s for immigration surveillance, and the Page Act of 1875 as the first exclusionary immigration law.**
> Congress in 1790 passed the first naturalization law for the United States, the Naturalization Act of 1790. [alce::-4561791635646149573::p1]
> The Alien and Sedition Acts of the 1790s is one of the earliest cases of immigration surveillance, allowing the president to act on their own discretion in regards to whether or not non-citizens were dangerous to the country because of their nationality. [alce::-4561791635646149573::p4]
> Congress passed its first exclusionary immigration law with the Page Act of 1875, which prohibited entry to immigrants from Asia that were thought of as undesirable, such as prostitutes and convicts. [alce::-4561791635646149573::p4]

Cited: alce::-4561791635646149573::p1, alce::-4561791635646149573::p4

### `6635099307335066088`
> **The International Red Cross was established in 1863, founded by Henry Dunant and others in response to the suffering witnessed at the Battle of Solferino in 1859.**
> Relief activist Henry Dunant witnessed the Battle of Solferino in 1859, fought between French-Piedmontese and Austrian armies in Northern Italy, where the subsequent suffering of 40,000 wounded soldiers left on the field due to lack of facilities, personnel, and truces to give them medical aid moved Dunant into action. [alce::6635099307335066088::p1]
> Dunant helped found the International Committee of the Red Cross in 1863. [alce::6635099307335066088::p1]
> From 26 to 29 October 1863, an international conference took place in Geneva to examine the practical implementation of Dunant's ideas. [alce::6635099307335066088::p4]

### `-8118284230798145822`
> **The Ottoman Empire took over Greece gradually from the late 14th century through the 15th century, with key dates including 1453 (Constantinople), 1458 (Athens), and 1460 (Peloponnese/Despotate of the Morea), completing the conquest of mainland Greece by 1460.**
> Most of Greece gradually became part of the Ottoman Empire in the late 14th and early 15th century, culminating in the Fall of Constantinople in 1453, the conquest of the Duchy of Athens in 1458, and of the Despotate of the Morea in 1460. [alce::-8118284230798145822::p3]
> The Ottomans captured Constantinople in 1453 and advanced southwards into Greece, capturing Athens in 1458, with the Greeks holding out in the Peloponnese until 1460. [alce::-8118284230798145822::p0]
> By 1500 most of the plains and islands of Greece were in Ottoman hands, though the Venetians and Genoese retained some islands, and Cyprus fell in 1571 while the Venetians retained Crete until 1670. [alce::-8118284230798145822::p0]

Cited: alce::-8118284230798145822::p0, alce::-8118284230798145822::p3

> Recent demographic studies have argued for a population peak ranging from (comparable to the contemporaneous and similarly sized Han empire in China), with one-tenth of them located in Italy itself, to more than. [alce::139979274734116034::p4]
> There are many estimates of the population for the Roman Empire, that range from 45 million to 120 million with 55-65 million as the most accepted range. [alce::139979274734116034::p3]

> **Southeast Texas (Piney Woods region and Golden Triangle area)**

### `3055530995120092075`
> **The Navigation Acts**
> The Navigation Acts, or more broadly The Acts of Trade and Navigation were a long series of English laws that developed, promoted, and regulated English ships, shipping, trade, and commerce between other countries and with its own colonies. [alce::3055530995120092075::p1]
> Parliament passed a series of laws called Navigation Acts that controlled trade within the British Empire. [alce::3055530995120092075::p2]
> Under the Navigation Acts, colonial goods could be carried only on English and colonial ships, and the American colonies were expected to export raw materials but not finished goods. [alce::3055530995120092075::p2]
> The Navigation Acts were first enacted in 1651 under the Commonwealth, reenacted and broadened with the restoration by the Act of 1660, and further developed and tightened by the Navigation Acts of 1663, 1673, and 1696. [alce::3055530995120092075::p1]

Cited: alce::3055530995120092075::p1, alce::3055530995120092075::p2

### `-3143111251154460535`
> **Nonpartisan blanket primary (jungle primary/top-two primary)**
> The non-partisan blanket primary (aka "jungle primary" or top-two primary) is a single primary election in which all voters may participate, and all candidates appear on the same ballot, regardless of party affiliation or lack of party affiliation. [alce::-3143111251154460535::p0]
> A nonpartisan blanket primary is a primary election in which all candidates for the same elected office, regardless of respective political party, run against each other at once, instead of being segregated by political party. [alce::-3143111251154460535::p2]
> A key difference between the Louisiana primary and a typical two-round system is that political parties do not select the individuals using their party labels; rather, candidates can self-identify using the label of their preferred political party (or no party at all). [alce::-3143111251154460535::p3]

Cited: alce::-3143111251154460535::p0, alce::-3143111251154460535::p2, alce::-3143111251154460535::p3

### `-8346671830214148499`
> **France stopped having kings and queens in 1792, with the monarchy being abolished on September 21, 1792, during the French Revolution. However, the monarchy was briefly restored in 1814 and lasted until 1848.**
> On 21 September 1792 the French monarchy was effectively abolished by the proclamation of the French First Republic. [alce::-8346671830214148499::p3]
> The monarchy was restored by the other great powers in 1814 and lasted (except for the Hundred Days in 1815) until the French Revolution of 1848. [alce::-8346671830214148499::p1]
> On 24 February 1848, the monarchy was abolished and the Second Republic was proclaimed, and despite later attempts to re-establish the Kingdom in the 1870s, during the Third Republic, the original French monarchy never returned. [alce::-8346671830214148499::p0]

Cited: alce::-8346671830214148499::p0, alce::-8346671830214148499::p1, alce::-8346671830214148499::p3

### `3071684398533564818`
> **There are multiple clock towers in London with different construction dates. The most famous is the Elizabeth Tower (Big Ben), but the sources provided discuss clock towers in Croydon (built 1892-1896), Leicester (constructed 1868), Cardiff Castle (built 1869-1873), and Dover Castle Clock (from about 1600).**
> The Clocktower in Croydon is the tower of the Town Hall, with the original town hall and library complex built in 1892–1896 to a design by Charles Henman. [alce::3071684398533564818::p1]
> The Clock Tower in Leicester was constructed in 1868. [alce::3071684398533564818::p2]
> The Clock Tower at Cardiff Castle was conceived in 1866 and planned by 1868, and was built between 1869-1873. [alce::3071684398533564818::p3]
> The Dover Castle Clock is a turret clock from the beginning of the 17th century, thought to date from about 1600. [alce::3071684398533564818::p4]

Cited: alce::3071684398533564818::p1, alce::3071684398533564818::p2, alce::3071684398533564818::p3, alce::3071684398533564818::p4

### `7133553793803549181`
> **Tennis Court Oath**
> On 20 June 1789, the members of the French Third Estate, who had begun to call themselves the National Assembly, took the Tennis Court Oath, vowing 'not to separate, and to reassemble wherever circumstances require, until the constitution of the kingdom is established'. [alce::7133553793803549181::p3]
> After finding the door to their chamber locked and guarded, the Assembly met nearby on a tennis court and pledged the Tennis Court Oath on 20 June 1789, binding them 'never to separate, and to meet wherever circumstances demand, until the constitution of the kingdom is established and affirmed on solid foundations.' They were joined by some sympathetic members of the Second and First estates. [alce::7133553793803549181::p4]

### `-5980018027276110696`
> **The authorship is disputed; traditionally attributed to Luke the companion of Paul, but modern scholars generally believe it was written by an anonymous Christian author**
> Traditionally, the text is believed to have been written by Luke the companion of Paul (named in Colossians). However, the earliest manuscripts are anonymous, and the traditional view has been challenged by many modern scholars. [alce::-5980018027276110696::p0]
> There is substantial evidence to indicate that the author of The Gospel of Luke also wrote the Book of Acts. [alce::-5980018027276110696::p0]
> Luke-Acts does not name its author. Church tradition identified him as Luke the Evangelist, the companion of Paul, but the majority of scholars reject this due to the many contradictions between Acts and the authentic Pauline letters. [alce::-5980018027276110696::p2]
> Many modern scholars have expressed doubt that the author of Luke-Acts was the physician Luke, and instead believe Luke-Acts was written by an anonymous Christian author who may not have been an eyewitness to any of the events recorded within the text. [alce::-5980018027276110696::p3]

Cited: alce::-5980018027276110696::p0, alce::-5980018027276110696::p2, alce::-5980018027276110696::p3

### `8664313149431483137`
> **The musculoskeletal system is an organ system that gives humans the ability to move and provides form, support, stability, and protection to the body**
> The human musculoskeletal system is an organ system that gives humans the ability to move using their muscular and skeletal systems. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> The musculoskeletal system provides form, support, stability, and movement to the body. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> It is made up of the bones of the skeleton, muscles, cartilage, tendons, ligaments, joints, and other connective tissue that supports and binds tissues and organs together. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]
> The musculoskeletal system's primary functions include supporting the body, allowing motion, and protecting vital organs. [alce::8664313149431483137::p0]

### `1986535647972136716`
> **Sperm is made in the testes**
> The seminiferous tubules of the testes are the starting point for the process, where spermatogonial stem cells adjacent to the inner tubule wall divide in a centripetal direction to produce immature sperm. [alce::1986535647972136716::p0]
> The developing sperm travel through the seminiferous tubules to the rete testis located in the mediastinum testis, to the efferent ducts, and then to the epididymis where newly created sperm cells mature. [alce::1986535647972136716::p2]
> The location [Testes/Scrotum] is specifically important as the process of spermatogenesis requires a lower temperature to produce viable sperm, specifically 1°-8 °C lower than normal body temperature of 37 °C. [alce::1986535647972136716::p0]

Cited: alce::1986535647972136716::p0, alce::1986535647972136716::p2
